narrative: On 1662, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Montreal, QC. The object was described as Fiery Serpents. Witness: Multiple settlers. Evidence: Celestial "omens". Source: Jesuit Relations.

On 1915, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Ottawa, ON. The object was described as Ghost Plane. Witness: Parliamentarians. Evidence: High-altitude lights during WWI. Source: Hansard Debates.

On 1947, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Goose Bay, NL. The object was described as Disc. Classification: RV (Hynek). Witness: Military Radar. Evidence: Detected at 9,000+ mph. Source: USAF Intel.

On 1950, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Rivers, MB. The object was described as Metallic Disc. Classification: RV (Hynek). Witness: RCAF Pilot. Evidence: High-speed maneuvers. Source: RCAF Pilot Logs.

media_urls.title: UFO craft drawing from witness description

narrative: On 1951, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Gander, NL. The object was described as Orange Circle. Classification: RV (Hynek). Witness: U.S. Navy Pilot. Evidence: Close proximity flight. Source: U.S. Navy Records.

On 1952, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near North Bay, ON. The object was described as Amber Orbs. Classification: RV (Hynek). Witness: RCAF Officers. Evidence: Radar-visual confirmation. Source: Project Magnet.

On 1954, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Ennadai Lake, NU. The object was described as Metallic Cylinder. Classification: DD (Hynek). Witness: Weather observers. Evidence: Metallic glint, no propulsion. Source: DND Declassified.

On 1960, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Clan Lake, NWT. The object was described as Sphere. Classification: CE2 (Hynek). Witness: 2 Prospectors. Evidence: Crashing sound; water disturbance. Source: RCMP Files.

witnesses: Prospector discovered scorched earth and small metallic fragment. Soil samples showed unusual isotopic ratios and localized radiation spikes.
physical_evidence: Disputed soil analysis reports (lost/suppressed by DND)
narrative: In 1960, a prospector near Yellowknife, NWT discovered an area of scorched earth and a small metallic fragment at a remote site. Soil samples reportedly showed unusual isotopic ratios and localized radiation spikes. The soil analysis reports were later described as lost or suppressed by the Department of National Defence. The case is one of several in Canada involving radioactive evidence at UFO-related sites, and has been connected to the nearby Clan Lake incident of the same year (CAN-008).
official_reports: MUFON — Physical Evidence case file, Canada-North division

narrative: On 1966, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Agassiz, BC. The object was described as Light Ship. Witness: Local residents. Evidence: Hovering over Mt. Cheam. Source: Local Legend/Press.

witnesses: A family witnessed multiple glowing spheres performing rigid right-angle turns over the White Rock/Surrey area during the massive 1966 Pacific Northwest flap. Metallic sheen in daylight, glowing at dusk. No sound reported.
physical_evidence: Original black-and-white photograph by local amateur photographer
narrative: In 1966, during the massive Pacific Northwest UFO flap, a family in the Surrey/White Rock area of British Columbia witnessed multiple glowing spheres performing rigid right-angle turns over their neighborhood. The objects had a metallic sheen in daylight and glowed at dusk. No sound was reported. Photographs were taken by a local amateur photographer and newspaper coverage was archived in the Vancouver Sun.
official_reports: MUFON — Early sighting archive, Pacific Northwest division

location_full: Falcon Lake, Whiteshell Provincial Park, MB
shape_detail: Spherical/saucer-shaped craft, approximately 12 feet (3.6m) diameter, 8 feet (2.4m) tall, made of metallic material resembling brushed steel with grid/mesh pattern
speed: Landed; later departed at high speed
being_type: N/A (Close Encounter 2nd Kind - Physical contact with craft)
being_behavior: Michalak saw two saucer-shaped objects in formation, landing side by side near Falcon Lake, Whiteshell Provincial Park. When he approached one, it emitted a flame/vapor and door opened. He leaned inside and was burned by heat from exhaust ports.
witness_credibility: High — documented, physical evidence, multiple corroborating sources
witness_occupation: Prospector, Amateur Scientist, Inventor from Winnipeg
physical_evidence: Severe burns on Stefan Michalak's torso (1-3 degree burns)
Burned shirt with grid-pattern matching craft ventilation slots
Radioactive soil samples from landing site
Piece of metal debris found near site (submitted for analysis)
electromagnetics: Radio interference reported; walkie-talkie reportedly malfunctioned during encounter
narrative: Stefan Michalak, a prospector and amateur scientist from Winnipeg, was rockhounding near Falcon Lake in the Whiteshell Provincial Park on May 20, 1967. He heard a whistling or humming sound, then saw two saucer-shaped objects in formation. The objects separated and one landed approximately 30 yards away. Michalak approached the craft and found it hot. He noticed a grid pattern of holes and what appeared to be ventilation slots. When he reached out to touch it, the object emitted a flame/vapor from an exhaust port, and a door opened. Michalak leaned inside and was knocked unconscious by a burst of intense heat. He awoke to find himself alone, with the craft gone. He suffered severe burns across his torso and abdomen. His shirt was burned, with a pattern matching the grid of holes in the craft. He experienced flu-like symptoms and was hospitalized. A piece of metal was found nearby. The RCMP investigated, and soil samples from the landing site were reportedly radioactive. Michalak's case was investigated by the Royal Canadian Mounted Police, the military, and civilian investigators. Dr. W.M. Klym of Manitoba's Department of Mines reportedly confirmed radiation levels. Michalak's story was widely reported and remains one of Canada's best-documented UFO cases with physical evidence.
radiation_details: Soil samples from landing site showed elevated radiation levels. Confirmed by Manitoba Department of Mines (Dr. W.M. Klym). Michalak's burned shirt showed trace radiation.
official_reports: Manitoba Department of Mines soil analysis

witnesses: Four teenage fishermen; RCMP officers Chris Oates and Ron Pound. DND divers dispatched. Object tracked underwater by sonar before vanishing.
narrative: On October 4, 1967, at approximately 11:20 PM, multiple witnesses observed a low-flying illuminated object descending toward the waters off Shag Harbour, Nova Scotia. Four teenage fishermen first spotted the object. Laurie Wickens and friends saw it approach the water, and Wickens called the RCMP to report a possible plane crash. RCMP officers arrived within 15 minutes to find an object floating in the water with lights visible, approximately 250-300 meters offshore. The object sank before rescue could be attempted. The RCMP, Canadian Coast Guard, RCAF, and Royal Canadian Navy all responded. Navy divers from Fleet Diving Unit Atlantic spent three days searching the seafloor. The RCMP officially filed a 'Flying Saucer' report — the only Canadian government document classifying an incident as a UFO. Yellowish foam was found on the water surface. Despite an extensive search involving naval vessels and divers, nothing was ever recovered. The Navy's final statement: 'Not a trace... not a clue... not a bit of anything.' This remains one of the most important Canadian UFO cases.

On 1967, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Shelburne, NS. The object was described as Disc. Classification: CE1 (Hynek). Witness: Military personnel. Evidence: Object entered the water. Source: Navy Radar Logs.

On 1967, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near St. Paul, AB. The object was described as Cultural. Witness: Town Council. Evidence: Centennial UFO Landing Pad built. Source: Municipal Records.

On 1968, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Rivers, MB. The object was described as Glowing Ring. Classification: RV (Hynek). Witness: Air Traffic Control. Evidence: Persistent radar track. Source: RCAF Base Records.

On 1969, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Prince George, BC. The object was described as Yellow Sphere. Classification: CE1 (Hynek). Witness: 3 Witnesses. Evidence: Hovering at low altitude. Source: RCMP Reports.

being_type: Humanoid — tall men in dark suits
witnesses: A nurse driving home from a late shift encountered a brilliant, silent light descending toward her vehicle. Car electronics failed, engine died. Brief missing time and vivid 'white room' experience. Vehicle required complete electrical diagnostic due to power surge.
physical_evidence: Automotive mechanic report citing 'impossible' voltage spike
electromagnetics: Car electronics failed; engine died; voltage surge on restart
narrative: On January 1, 1970, nurse Doreen Kendall was driving home from a late shift at Duncan Hospital, Vancouver Island, when she encountered a brilliant, silent light that descended toward her vehicle. Her car's electronics immediately failed and the engine died. During the encounter, she experienced what she described as missing time and a vivid 'white room' experience involving two tall beings in dark suits. Upon regaining consciousness, she found her car's electrical system completely drained — the mechanic later described the voltage spike as 'impossible' from any known vehicle malfunction. The case was later documented by researcher Chris Rutkowski and classified as a Close Encounter of the Third Kind.
official_reports: Documented in Chris Rutkowski's 'Canadian UFOs: The National Report'

narrative: On 1972, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Oromocto, NB. The object was described as Silver Discs. Classification: CE1 (Hynek). Witness: Military (CFB Gagetown). Evidence: Multiple discs in formation. Source: CFB Gagetown.

On 1973, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Val-d'Or, QC. The object was described as Multi-colored. Classification: CE1 (Hynek). Witness: Police/Public. Evidence: Massive regional "wave". Source: Ufology Quebec.

On 1973, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Granby, QC. The object was described as Glowing Oval. Classification: CE3 (Hynek). Witness: Anonymous. Evidence: Small humanoids reported. Source: Quebec Archives.

On 1974, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Langenburg, SK. The object was described as 5 Saucers. Classification: CE2 (Hynek). Witness: Edwin Fuhr. Evidence: 5 rings of flattened/swirled grass. Source: RCMP Report.

On 1975, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Carman, MB. The object was described as Red Light. Witness: "Charlie Redstar". Evidence: 1,000s of sightings over months. Source: Charlie Redstar (Grant).

On 1977, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Montreal, QC. The object was described as Saucer. Classification: CE2 (Hynek). Witness: Florida Malboeuf. Evidence: Landing gear marks in snow. Source: Ufology Research.

On 1978, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Clarenville, NL. The object was described as Oval. Classification: CE1 (Hynek). Witness: Cst. Jim Blackwood. Evidence: Hovering light for 1.5 hours. Source: RCMP Witness.

On 1978, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Baskatong, QC. The object was described as Saucer. Classification: DD (Hynek). Witness: Jacques Lavoie. Evidence: Detailed 35mm photograph. Source: Photo Analysis.

On 1979, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Saddle Lake, AB. The object was described as Triangle. Classification: CE1 (Hynek). Witness: First Nations Community. Evidence: Silent, low-level flight. Source: Community Logs.

witnesses: Military personnel at CFB Portage la Prairie. Large metallic cigar-shaped object hovered over runway for 20+ minutes. Searchlights seemed to bend around it. Object departed at extreme velocity.
radar_confirmation: Yes — base radar tracked the object
narrative: In 1979, military personnel at CFB Portage la Prairie, Manitoba reported a large metallic cigar-shaped object hovering over the base runway for over 20 minutes. Base searchlights were reportedly directed at the object, but the light appeared to bend or pass around it without illuminating it clearly. The object was tracked on base radar. It eventually departed at extreme velocity, disappearing from radar in seconds. The incident was documented in MUFON files and the Winnipeg Sun archives.
official_reports: MUFON — Archived under Manitoba Military Files 1979

narrative: On 1980, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Nahanni Valley, NT. The object was described as Glowing Orbs. Witness: Adventurers. Evidence: Orbs following hikers. Source: "Headless Valley" Lore.

witnesses: Quebec provincial police (SQ) officers on highway patrol witnessed a hovering, disc-shaped object with rotating multicolored lights. Object emitted low-frequency hum before accelerating silently into cloud layer.
physical_evidence: Joint affidavit signed by two officers
narrative: In 1987, two Quebec provincial police (Sureté du Quebec) officers on highway patrol near L'Ancienne-Lorette, Quebec witnessed a hovering disc-shaped object with rotating multicolored lights. The object emitted a low-frequency hum before accelerating silently upward and disappearing into the cloud layer. Both officers signed a joint affidavit and filed an official SQ incident report. Law enforcement witnesses give this case high credibility.
official_reports: SQ — Official SQ incident report logs

narrative: On 1981, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Kelowna, BC. The object was described as Silver Sphere. Classification: DD (Hynek). Witness: Local resident. Evidence: Daylight photo of sphere. Source: Photographic Evidence.

On 1985, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Westmount, QC. The object was described as Rectangle. Witness: Thousands of locals. Evidence: Massive silent block of lights. Source: Montreal Gazetter.

On 1989, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Shelburne, NS. The object was described as Triangle. Classification: CE1 (Hynek). Witness: Fishermen. Evidence: Large craft surfacing from water. Source: Maritime UFO Logs.

physical_evidence: Seen over Place Bonaventure for 3 hours
narrative: On Nov 7, 1990, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Montreal, QC. The object was described as Oval/Luminous. Classification: CE1 (Hynek). Witness: Police & 40+ people. Evidence: Seen over Place Bonaventure for 3 hours. Source: Montreal Police.

On 1991, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Carp, ON. The object was described as Red Flame/Craft. Classification: CE3 (Hynek). Witness: Diane Labenek. Evidence: "The Guardian" VHS tapes; scorched field. Source: MUFON/Bob Oechsler.

On 1991, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near St-Zotique, QC. The object was described as Metallic Disc. Classification: DD (Hynek). Witness: Multiple witnesses. Evidence: High-resolution daylight photo. Source: Photo Analysis.

On 1992, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near l'Annonciation, QC. The object was described as Craft. Classification: CE3 (Hynek). Witness: Snowmobilers. Evidence: Video of supposed "corpse" (likely hoax). Source: Ufology Archives.

On 1993, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Port Colborne, ON. The object was described as Bright Light. Classification: CE4 (Hynek). Witness: 1 Witness. Evidence: Missing time; hypnotic regression. Source: Witness Account.

On 1994, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near St-Jean-sur-Ric.. The object was described as Oval. Classification: CE2 (Hynek). Witness: Local farmer. Evidence: Dehydrated vegetation/ground marks. Source: Trace Case.

On 1996, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Fox Lake, YT. The object was described as Mothership. Classification: CE1 (Hynek). Witness: 31 Witnesses. Evidence: Massive craft (3 football fields wide). Source: Yukon Govt Files.

On 2004, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Fort Erie, ON. The object was described as Vibrating Lights. Witness: Multi-witness. Evidence: Lights dancing over Lake Erie. Source: Ufology Research.

On 2007, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near St-Hilaire, QC. The object was described as Glowing Orb. Witness: Residents. Evidence: Orb emerging from Mont St-Hilaire. Source: Local Reports.

being_appearance: Men in dark, dated suits; unnatural pale complexions
witnesses: Local resident witnessed a triangular craft hovering silently above the falls. 72 hours later, two men in dark, dated suits (Men in Black) visited the home. Warned witness not to discuss sighting. Unnatural pale complexions.
physical_evidence: Witness audio interview recorded by MUFON investigators
Sketch of the alleged Men in Black
narrative: In 2008, a hotel security guard in Niagara Falls, Ontario witnessed a triangular craft hovering silently above the falls. Approximately 72 hours later, two men in dark, dated suits with unnatural pale complexions visited the witness's home and warned them not to discuss the sighting. The case was documented in MUFON's MIB Encounter Database as Case #94-ONF.

witnesses: Dozens of residents. 14-year-old boy reported low-altitude V-shaped craft. CFB Goose Bay military radar tracked objects at impossible speeds/altitudes. RCMP Const. Paul Snow confirmed 30+ credible reports.
narrative: Beginning on January 5, 2008, dozens of residents of Stephenville, Newfoundland reported seeing large lights moving silently across the sky. A 14-year-old boy reported a V-shaped craft flying at extremely low altitude over the town. The U.S. military acknowledged operating aircraft from CFB Goose Bay that evening but denied having any craft that would account for all the sightings. CFB Goose Bay radar reportedly tracked objects moving at speeds exceeding 1,000 mph at altitudes of 500-5,000 feet — well outside normal aircraft operating parameters. RCMP Constable Paul Snow confirmed over 30 credible reports in the following days. The case gained international attention and was dubbed the 'Newfoundland Trench' incident. Military radar tracks at impossible speeds combined with dozens of civilian and police reports make this one of the most compelling modern Canadian UFO cases.

On 2010, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Harbour Mille, NL. The object was described as Missiles. Classification: CE1 (Hynek). Witness: Multi-witness. Evidence: 3 projectile-like objects from water. Source: PMO/RCMP Inquiry.

witnesses: Multiple witnesses near 34th Canadian Brigade Group base reported massive, slow-moving triangular lights. Complete radio silence, localized EM interference. Base lockdown protocols allegedly initiated.
electromagnetics: Localized EM interference reported near base
narrative: In October 2008, multiple witnesses near CFB Valcartier, a Canadian Forces Base north of Quebec City, reported a massive, slow-moving triangular craft with lights at each corner. The object was described as silent and moving at low altitude over the military installation. Base lockdown protocols were allegedly initiated in response. The incident was reported to several Quebec UFO databases and gained coverage in French-language media. The proximity to a major military base and the alleged base response elevated this case above typical civilian sightings.

On 2012, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Hamilton, ON. The object was described as V-Formation. Witness: Dozens of witnesses. Evidence: High-quality YouTube video uploads. Source: Multi-source.

On 2014, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Vancouver, BC. The object was described as Mechanical. Classification: DD (Hynek). Witness: Commercial Pilot. Evidence: Near-miss at YVR airport. Source: CADORS.

On 2015, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Oshawa, ON. The object was described as Black Cube. Classification: DD (Hynek). Witness: Amateur Video. Evidence: Cube rotating in clear sky. Source: Video Evidence.

On 2016, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Ashmount, AB. The object was described as Bright Orb. Classification: CE1 (Hynek). Witness: Oil workers. Evidence: Electromagnetic interference with trucks. Source: Field Reports.

On 2021, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Gulf of St. Law.. The object was described as Green Light. Witness: Military/Commercial. Evidence: Verified by two different aircraft. Source: CADORS 2021Q2032.

On 2024, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Hafford, SK. The object was described as Domed Disc. Classification: CE1 (Hynek). Witness: Single witness. Evidence: Low altitude hover over field. Source: MUFON Case 13582.

On 2025, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Sudbury, ON. The object was described as Orange Diamond. Witness: 4 Witnesses. Evidence: Pulsating orange light over mines. Source: 2025 Ufology Report.

On 2025, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near N. Alberta. The object was described as Cylinder. Classification: RV (Hynek). Witness: Pilot. Evidence: Incident at 39,000ft; high speed. Source: CADORS (Projected).

witnesses: Commercial pilot reported fast-moving, non-transponder object pacing aircraft at parallel altitude for 12 miles. Object executed impossible vertical climb and vanished from radar. ATC confirmed no scheduled military exercises.
radar_confirmation: Yes — ATC radar tracked the object
narrative: In February 2025, a commercial pilot on approach over Vulcan County, Alberta reported a fast-moving, non-transponder object pacing the aircraft at a parallel altitude for approximately 12 miles. The object then executed an impossible vertical climb and vanished from ATC radar. Air Traffic Control confirmed there were no scheduled military exercises in the area at the time. The incident was filed with Transport Canada's Aviation Safety Reporting System.
official_reports: Transport Canada — ASRS (Aviation Safety Reporting System) filing

narrative: On 2025, one or more witnesses reported an unidentified aerial phenomenon near Sheridan Lake, BC. The object was described as Pulsating Orb. Witness: Campers. Evidence: Interference with cell phones. Source: Canadian UFO Survey.

On June 4, 2014, John Sheppard was putting out a bonfire at his property near Kensington, Prince Edward Island, when he noticed unusual lights over the Gulf of St. Lawrence. The lights moved in an erratic pattern across the night sky, performing maneuvers inconsistent with conventional aircraft. Sheppard captured approximately 22 minutes of video footage on his cellphone, showing the lights changing direction and intensity. The case was reported to the Mutual UFO Network (MUFON), which confirmed the sighting. CBC News covered the incident, with follow-up analysis offering potential alternate explanations. The Kensington Lights remain one of the few documented UFO cases from Prince Edward Island.

On July 1, 1997 (Canada Day), two women who were strangers to each other were waiting for fireworks on the Columbia River bridge in Trail, British Columbia, when they saw three small lights above them. The lights descended to approximately 1-2 feet above the water surface, then zig-zagged eastbound along the river before flying out of sight. It was still daylight and the fireworks had not yet started. Both witnesses independently reported the same observations, with no prior connection between them.

On July 26, 2014, from approximately 7:00 to 10:00 PM, multiple reports came in of a string of 6-7 diagonal flashing lights over North York, Toronto. Sarah Chun recorded two videos from her condo balcony and posted them to YouTube. Toronto Police 32 Division received several calls from concerned residents. Multiple police officers witnessed the lights, with one speculating it might be a quadcopter, though no investigation was conducted.

A sustained wave of UFO sightings swept across southern Manitoba over a multi-month period in 1975-1976. Multiple RCMP reports were filed from various communities. The wave included the Carman (CAN-023) and Portage la Prairie (CAN-028) incidents, plus additional unreported cases documented by Chris Rutkowski and Ufology Research of Manitoba. The flap pattern suggests a regional phenomenon rather than isolated events.

In 2002, northern British Columbia recorded 25 UFO sightings in and around Terrace — the highest per-capita rate in Canada, according to the Canadian UFO Survey conducted by Chris Rutkowski and Ufology Research of Manitoba. Multiple distinct reports featured various shapes and behaviours, suggesting an active period of unidentified aerial phenomena in the region.

In November 1989, thousands of witnesses across Montreal reported a massive UFO hovering over the city. The object was described as a large boomerang-shaped craft with lights along its edges. The sighting lasted approximately one hour and was covered extensively in Montreal media. It remains one of the largest mass sightings in Canadian history and is currently not represented in any other HEIMDALL case.

In 1975, multiple witnesses in Dawson Creek, British Columbia reported a large cigar-shaped object sighted over the town. The object was described as metallic, silent, with a row of lights along its underside. It hovered for several minutes before accelerating rapidly out of sight. RCMP received and documented reports from multiple callers.

In 1966, a physical trace case was reported near Cranbrook, British Columbia. Witnesses reported a landed craft with clear landing gear impressions found pressed into the soil at the site. The pattern was similar to the CAN-022 Langenburg incident from Saskatchewan. RCMP investigated the site and documented the physical evidence, though no definitive explanation was reached.

In 1996, a Close Encounter of the Third Kind was reported near St. Stephen, New Brunswick. Witnesses reported a landed craft with a humanoid figure visible nearby. The case is one of the more detailed entity encounter cases from Atlantic Canada and would be the second New Brunswick case in the HEIMDALL dataset after Oromocto (CAN-019).

In the weeks following the January 25, 2010 missile-shaped UFO event (CAN-045), additional sightings were reported in the Harbour Mille, Newfoundland area. Multiple witnesses came forward with reports of similar objects, and RCMP took statements. The follow-up sightings expanded the original event from a single sighting into a broader phenomenon.

In 1969, multiple witnesses near Gimli, Manitoba reported orange lights performing aerial manoeuvres over Lake Winnipeg. RCMP were involved in documenting the sightings. The event is part of the larger Manitoba flap pattern of the late 1960s, which also includes other HEIMDALL cases from the province.

In 1973, a Close Encounter of the Second Kind was reported near Bonnyville, Alberta. Physical traces were found at the landing site, including burned vegetation and ground impressions. RCMP investigated the site and documented the evidence. The case adds to Alberta's currently thin UFO coverage of only 4 cases in the main dataset.

physical_evidence: Missing time after rural road encounter
narrative: In 1994, a driver on Rama Road near Orillia, Ontario reported missing time and unusual memories after encountering a bright light on a rural road at night. The witness described a period of unaccounted time and fragmented recollections of being examined. The case would be only the second CE4 (abduction) case in the HEIMDALL dataset after CAN-043.

In 2022, multiple UFO sightings were reported near Yellowknife, Northwest Territories. The Department of National Defence tracked some of these objects and confirmed they did not correspond to known aircraft or military exercises. The case adds a modern data point to the underrepresented Northwest Territories, complementing the historical 1960 Yellowknife case (CAN-009).
